title: A Machine-Learning Based Approach to the Evaluation of the Critical Scaling Behavior of Anisotropic Spin Systems
authors: Alina A. Chubarova, Ivan A. Mamonov, Marina V. Mamonova, Mikhail I. Bogachev, Pavel V. Prudnikov
abstract: Computational models adequately representing phase transitions and evaluating the critical system parameters are essential for the understanding of the properties of a wide range of materials. Here we propose a machine learning (ML)-based approach to the identification of the critical point in anisotropic spin systems. Our approach implies training of a convolutional neural network (CNN) model from the correlation matrices obtained by Monte Carlo simulations. Next, the pretrained model is employed as a fast estimator of the critical temperature, which can be extracted in several complementary ways from the CNN model inference, this way improving the robustness of the analysis. The ML-based estimates obtained in this study are in very good agreement with the reference Monte Carlo simulation results, while computational costs are about 10x lower compared to the classical thermodynamic approach.
summary: This paper proposes a novel machine learning framework using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to pinpoint the critical temperature in anisotropic spin systems. By training on correlation matrices from Monte Carlo simulations, the method significantly reduces computational overhead while maintaining high accuracy compared to traditional thermodynamic calculations. This demonstrates a powerful synergy between deep learning and condensed matter physics for studying phase transitions.
main_problem: The study aims to evaluate the critical scaling behavior and identify the critical point ($T_c$) in anisotropic spin systems.
main_result: The machine learning approach provides estimates for $T_c$ that show very good agreement with reference Monte Carlo simulations while achieving a computational cost reduction of about 10x compared to classical methods.
method: A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is trained on correlation matrices derived from Monte Carlo simulations. The critical temperature is then estimated using multiple inference techniques applied to the CNN's probabilistic output.
model_or_system: The system modeled is a three-dimensional Heisenberg spin system exhibiting easy-axis anisotropy, described by a specific Hamiltonian involving exchange interactions ($J$) and anisotropy ($\Delta$).
key_observables: Correlation function $C_i(T)$, averaged output probabilities $P(T, L)$, deviation functions $E_r(T, L)$, and correlation-length-like quantities $\xi$.
important_parameters:
The critical exponent ($
u$) is determined to be approximately 0.58(1). The analysis depends on the system size ($L$) and temperature discretization.
main_assumptions: The accuracy of the ML method relies heavily on the quality and density of the training data, particularly requiring fine temperature sampling near the critical region.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate correlation matrix changes across temperatures, the CNN architecture, and how varying the temperature sampling grid affects the sharpness of the transition observed in probability curves.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem, details the ML/CNN methodology using correlation matrices as input, presents multiple estimators for $T_c$ (crossing points, peak positions), validates results against classical Monte Carlo simulations, and concludes with finite-size scaling analysis to determine critical exponents.
why_interesting: While focused on spin systems, the core methodology—using deep learning to extract complex physical parameters from high-dimensional simulation data—is highly relevant for analyzing quantum many-body dynamics or phase transitions in simulated quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
